[JS Weird World] No.54 A Little Danger Warning: Built-in Function Constructors

We've already covered built-in function constructors in earlier chapters, but they've got some dangerous spots too. They're nice and concise, but that comes with traps. This chapter is mainly about why built-in function constructors get risky when they're handling primitives, booleans, numbers, and strings.

, created through the built-in constructor, is an object.
It's a primitive. So under double equals, JavaScript coerces both sides to the same type, but under triple equals you get
. Which tells us that a primitive built through a built-in function constructor isn't actually a real primitive. So we can wrap up with a takeaway here.
Generally speaking, it's better not to use built-in function constructors — just use the literal syntax.
If you really do need a built-in function constructor, you need to know exactly what you're doing, like the type differences involved.
